title: Verilator for Single-Event Effect fault injection
body:
I would like to use Verilator to perform Single-Event Effect (SEE) fault injection, such as Single-Event Upsets in flip-flops and Single-Event Transients in gates*, to determine a failure rate on a functional level.

### Background
Algorithmic or probabilistic fault analysis techniques have the problem that they analyze a static design and only calculate the fault propagation. In order to take the functional level of a design into account one has to perform fault injections during a circuit simulation. The first principle method to achieve this is to simulate the design (RTL or GLN) with a testbench representing a realistic workload and then randomly chose a target instance and time to inject the fault. The simulation is continued and it is evaluated if a failures can be observed appear on the functional level. However, this method can become very expensive in terms of computing resources and tool licenses. 

I was amazed by the speed of Verilator and was hoping I could develop a fault injection procedure for it. The additional fact that it is open source and free, thus does not require any licenses, makes it also very attractive to parallelize the fault injection simulations on a large scale (e.g. one parallel simulation per target flop-flop and injection time).

### Implementation Details

I started with a simple purely combinatorial circuit, the c17 from the ISCAS85 benchmark suite, and tried to inject Single-Event Transients. The SET model is simplified by just inverting the output of the target gate during the injection time/cycle. I verilated the module and build a small testbench in C++ iterating over all possible input values. For each input combination I invert the value of the target gate output and compare the module output against a fault-free reference.

In my first attempt I was able to modify the circuit and add a saboteur which modifies the target gate's output net (see attached files). The net is simply inverted, depending if the fault injection is enabled or not 
`xor XOR(trg_net_fault, trg_net, fi_en)`
Although, this would be suitable to do for every net in this small circuit and maybe even scriptable for bigger circuits (such as other ISCAS85 benchmarks), I imagine it is getting quite complex for big designs with several hierarchies. This is why to my opinion, the ideal implementation would be build within the testbench, keeping the circuit untouched.

When I tried to modify the target net from the testbench (see attached files), I noticed the difference between event driven and cycle based simulators (at least that is my understanding). Although the modified net seems to be taken over it does not propagate to the output. When I call the evaluation function of the module again, I guess the modified net gets overwritten by the previous drivers. I tried similar thing with VPI and the vpi_put_value function, but it seems to be the same problem.

**Do you have an idea how I could achieve the described fault injection behavior and could point me in the right direction?**

*I am aware that Verilator is not able to perform timing aware simulation and transients with different pulse length could not be simulated. However, the transient pulse can still be modeled in more abstract way and the propagation and effect on a functional level can still be analyzed. This analysis could be paired with a lower level electrical and timing analysis.

[verilator_fi.tar.gz](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/files/4395458/verilator_fi.tar.gz)
